real_meaning: ～そうだ has two completely distinct uses that learners must keep separate: (1) <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby>そうだ — 'it looks/seems like ~' based on direct visual or sensory evidence right in front of you, expressing your own judgment; (2) <ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby>そうだ — 'I heard that ~' / 'apparently ~', reporting information you received from someone else or from a source. The speaker's relationship to the information is completely different in each case.

formation.rule: <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby> (looks like): verb stem + そうだ (e.g., <ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうだ), い-adj minus い + そうだ (e.g., <ruby>美味<rt>おい</rt></ruby>しそうだ), な-adj stem + そうだ (e.g., <ruby>元気<rt>げんき</rt></ruby>そうだ). <ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby> (hearsay): plain form of verb/adj/noun+だ + そうだ (e.g., <ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうだ、<ruby>美味<rt>おい</rt></ruby>しいそうだ、<ruby>元気<rt>げんき</rt></ruby>だそうだ).
formation.common_mistake: Using the full dictionary form for <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby>そうだ — learners write ✗ <ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうだ when they mean 'it looks like it will rain' (that's actually the hearsay form). The correct <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby> form is ✓ <ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうだ (verb stem only). Also watch out for いい → よさそうだ (not ✗ いいそうだ) and ない → なさそうだ (not ✗ なさいそうだ).

sentences.jp: この<ruby>荷物<rt>にもつ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>重<rt>おも</rt></ruby>そうだね。<ruby>手伝<rt>てつだ</rt></ruby>おうか？
sentences.translation: That bag looks heavy. Want me to help?
sentences.notes: Classic <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby>そうだ — the speaker is looking directly at the bag and making a sensory judgment, not reporting what someone told them.

sentences.jp: <ruby>天気<rt>てんき</rt></ruby><ruby>予報<rt>よほう</rt></ruby>によると、<ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>は<ruby>雪<rt>ゆき</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうですよ。
sentences.translation: According to the weather forecast, it's going to snow tomorrow.
sentences.notes: <ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby>そうだ — the speaker heard this from an external source (the forecast), and によると is a natural companion phrase that signals hearsay.

sentences.jp: <ruby>田中<rt>たなか</rt></ruby><ruby>部長<rt>ぶちょう</rt></ruby>は<ruby>来月<rt>らいげつ</rt></ruby>ご<ruby>退職<rt>たいしょく</rt></ruby>になるそうでございます。
sentences.translation: I understand that Section Chief Tanaka will be retiring next month.
sentences.notes: <ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby>そうだ in formal speech — the speaker is distancing themselves from direct responsibility for the information, which is often a polite or cautious move in workplace Japanese.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>店<rt>みせ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>潰<rt>つぶ</rt></ruby>れそうで、<ruby>潰<rt>つぶ</rt></ruby>れないんだよな。
sentences.translation: That place always looks like it's about to go under, but it never does.
sentences.notes: The contrast between そうで (looks like it will) and the reality (it doesn't) shows how <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby>そうだ captures appearances that can be misleading — a nuance textbooks rarely highlight.

confused_with.contrast: Both can express 'apparently' but the evidence source differs. ～そうだ (<ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby>) reports a specific, traceable piece of information — someone told you, you read it. ～らしい signals a vaguer inference from general signs, rumors, or indirect clues. Minimal pair: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>結婚<rt>けっこん</rt></ruby>したそうだ (I heard she got married — someone told me specifically) vs. <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>結婚<rt>けっこん</rt></ruby>したらしい (She seems to have gotten married — I picked this up from various indirect signs). Use そうだ when you can point to a source; use らしい when it's more of a 'word on the street' feeling.

bunpro_tip: Train yourself to check the verb form first: verb STEM + そうだ = eyes-on 'looks like'; PLAIN FORM + そうだ = 'I heard'. On BunPro, if the hint mentions seeing something or the sentence is about appearance, go <ruby>様態<rt>ようたい</rt></ruby> (stem); if it mentions a source or reported info, go <ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby> (plain form).
